title: Multi-Objective Prioritization with the Epsilon-Constraint Approach
text: Systematic conservation planning framework identify priority areas conservation management (Giakoumi et al. 2025). Briefly, priority areas identified formulating mathematical optimization problem based conservation objectives requirements using optimization algorithms generate solutions. help account multiple objectives, moec.prioritizr R package provides implementation epsilon-constraint approach multi-objective optimization (Eichfelder 2008). package designed plugin prioritizr R package – general purpose package systematic conservation planning (Hanson et al. 2025) – provide enhanced functionality. using multi-objective optimization approaches, conservation scientists practitioners can explore trade-offs identify solutions represent desirable compromise among multiple objectives (Neubert et al. 2025).

title: Multi-Objective Prioritization with the Epsilon-Constraint Approach
text: provide short example using moec.prioritizr R package generate conservation plans (hereafter, prioritizations) prioritizr R package. Specifically, use example dataset available prioritizrdata R package. Additionally, use terra R package perform raster calculations ggplot2 R package visualizations. begin , load packages. use Washington dataset example. import planning unit data, use get_wa_pu() function. Although prioritizr R package can support many different types planning unit data, planning units represented single-layer raster (.e., terra::rast() object). cell represents different planning unit, cell values denote land acquisition costs. Specifically, 10757 planning units total (.e., cells non-missing values).  also import data existing 0rotected areas. achieve , use get_wa_locked_in() function import spatially explicit protected area data single-layer raster, wherein cell values denote locations existing protected areas (values 1 indicate existing protected areas).  now import data native bird species. use get_wa_species() function import species distribution data multi-layer raster, wherein layer corresponds different species cell values denote relative abundance individuals (higher values mean greater abundance). account migratory patterns, breeding non-breeding distributions species represented different layers. Additionally, use get_wa_attr() function import additional information species tabular format (.e., tibble::tibble() object), wherein row corresponds different layer species distribution data. extinction_prob column contains probability extinction interest_score column contains measure public interest species. Since public interest scores contain numbers range zero high numbers, rescale values range 0 1 assist interpretation.  Next, import data vulnerable carbon. Since habitat destruction human activities can release sequestered carbon atmosphere, establishing protected areas places high vulnerable carbon can help reduce carbon emissions. use get_wa_carbon() function import spatially explicit data vulnerable carbon single-layer raster, wherein cell values denote total amount carbon vulnerable human activities.  importing data, let’s make sure solver installed computer. important can use optimization algorithms generate spatial prioritizations. first time using prioritizr R package, please install HiGHS solver using following R code. Although HiGHS solver relatively fast easy install, please note ’ll need install Gurobi software suite gurobi R package best performance (see Gurobi Installation Guide details). Now, let’s generate spatial prioritization identify priority areas protected areas establishment. particular, want identify priority areas seek promote representation () threatened species (based extinction probability, per wa_attr$extinction_prob), (ii) public interest species (based public interest, per wa_attr$interest_score), (iii) vulnerable carbon. consider well species represented, consider species adequately represented least 40% distributed covered existing protected areas priority areas (words, consider relative targets 40%). Additionally, use locked constraints existing protected areas selected prioritization ensure priority areas complement existing protected areas. Furthermore, set budgets ensure total cost existing protected areas priority areas exceed 3% total costs across study area. Since multiple conservation objectives want consider, use epsilon-constraint approach multi-objective optimization. particular approach especially well-suited generating set solutions characterize spectrum trade-offs among multiple objective. information building conservation planning problems (.e., problem() multi_problem()), please refer prioritizr R package documentation. generating prioritizations, now visualize spatial distribution priority areas.  can also calculate metrics examine trade-offs different prioritizations.
code: # to install packages for this example, please use: # install.packages(c("prioritizr", "prioritizrdata", "terra", "ggplot2"))  # load packages library(prioritizr) library(prioritizrdata) library(terra) library(ggplot2) # import planning unit data wa_pu <- get_wa_pu()  # preview data print(wa_pu) ## class       : SpatRaster ## size        : 109, 147, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) ## resolution  : 4000, 4000  (x, y) ## extent      : -1816382, -1228382, 247483.5, 683483.5  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) ## coord. ref. : +proj=laea +lat_0=45 +lon_0=-100 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +ellps=sphere +units=m +no_defs ## source      : wa_pu.tif ## name        :        cost ## min value   :    0.298665 ## max value   : 1804.183838 # plot data plot(wa_pu, main = "Costs", axes = FALSE) # import existing protected area data wa_locked_in <- get_wa_locked_in()  # preview data print(wa_locked_in) ## class       : SpatRaster ## size        : 109, 147, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) ## resolution  : 4000, 4000  (x, y) ## extent      : -1816382, -1228382, 247483.5, 683483.5  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) ## coord. ref. : +proj=laea +lat_0=45 +lon_0=-100 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +ellps=sphere +units=m +no_defs ## source      : wa_locked_in.tif ## name        : protected areas ## min value   :               0 ## max value   :               1 # plot data plot(wa_locked_in, main = "Existing protected areas", axes = FALSE) # import data wa_species <- get_wa_species() wa_attr <- get_wa_attr()  # rescale public interest scores, # note we use log10 transformation due to very high values and add a # value of 0.01 so that all species have a non-zero value wa_attr$interest_score <- log10(wa_attr$interest_score + 1) + 0.01  # preview data print(wa_species) ## class       : SpatRaster ## size        : 109, 147, 396  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) ## resolution  : 4000, 4000  (x, y) ## extent      : -1816382, -1228382, 247483.5, 683483.5  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) ## coord. ref. : +proj=laea +lat_0=45 +lon_0=-100 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +ellps=sphere +units=m +no_defs ## source      : wa_features.tif ## names       : Recur~ding), Botau~ding), Botau~ding), Corvu~ding), Corvu~ding), Cincl~full), ... ## min values  :           0,           0,           0,           0,           0,           0, ... ## max values  :       0.514,       0.812,       3.129,       0.115,       0.296,        0.06, ... print(wa_attr) ## # A tibble: 396 × 6 ##    feature                  binomial family order extinction_prob interest_score ##    <chr>                    <chr>    <chr>  <chr>           <dbl>          <dbl> ##  1 Recurvirostra americana… Recurvi… Recur… Char…          0.0009          0.01  ##  2 Botaurus lentiginosus (… Botauru… Ardei… Pele…          0.0009          0.311 ##  3 Botaurus lentiginosus (… Botauru… Ardei… Pele…          0.0009          0.311 ##  4 Corvus brachyrhynchos (… Corvus … Corvi… Pass…          0.0009          0.01  ##  5 Corvus brachyrhynchos (… Corvus … Corvi… Pass…          0.0009          0.01  ##  6 Cinclus mexicanus (full) Cinclus… Cincl… Pass…          0.0009          0.01  ##  7 Spinus tristis (breedin… Spinus … Fring… Pass…          0.0009          0.01  ##  8 Spinus tristis (nonbree… Spinus … Fring… Pass…          0.0009          0.01  ##  9 Falco sparverius (breed… Falco s… Falco… Falc…          0.0009          3.73  ## 10 Falco sparverius (nonbr… Falco s… Falco… Falc…          0.0009          3.73  ## # ℹ 386 more rows # plot the first nine layers of species distribution data plot(wa_species[[1:9]], nr = 3, axes = FALSE) # import data wa_carbon <- get_wa_carbon()  # preview data print(wa_carbon) ## class       : SpatRaster ## size        : 109, 147, 1  (nrow, ncol, nlyr) ## resolution  : 4000, 4000  (x, y) ## extent      : -1816382, -1228382, 247483.5, 683483.5  (xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax) ## coord. ref. : +proj=laea +lat_0=45 +lon_0=-100 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +ellps=sphere +units=m +no_defs ## source      : wa_carbon.tif ## name        : vulnerable carbon ## min value   :                 0 ## max value   :        266.039307 # plot data plot(wa_carbon, main = "Carbon", axes = FALSE) # if needed, install HiGHS solver install.packages("highs", repos = "https://cran.rstudio.com/") # define a total conservation budget (3% of total cost) budget <- 0.03 * terra::global(wa_pu, "sum", na.rm = TRUE)[[1]]  # create single-objective problem for threatened species p1 <-   problem(wa_pu, wa_species) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget = budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.8) %>%   add_feature_weights(wa_attr$extinction_prob) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # create single-objective problem for public interest species p2 <-   problem(wa_pu, wa_species) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget = budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.8) %>%   add_feature_weights(wa_attr$interest_score) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # create single-objective problem for vulnerable carbon, # note that we only need to specify the constraints for one of the three # problems because all constraints will be considered throughout the # entire multi-objective optimization process p3 <-   problem(wa_pu, wa_carbon) %>%   add_max_wtd_sum_objective(budget = budget) %>%   add_locked_in_constraints(wa_locked_in) %>%   add_binary_decisions() # now create multi-objective problem with epsilon-constraint approach, # and specify settings to generate a suite of different prioritizations mp <-   multi_problem(     threatened_species = p1,     public_interest_species = p2,     vulnerable_carbon = p3   ) %>%   add_eps_constraint_approach(n_per_problem = 3, verbose = TRUE) %>%   add_default_solver(verbose = FALSE)  # print multi-objective problem print(mp) ## A multi-objective conservation problem (<MultiConservationProblem>) ## ├•data ## │└•planning units: ## │ ├•data:       <SpatRaster> (10757 total) ## │ ├•extent:     -1816382, 247483.5, -1228382, 683483.5 (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax) ## │ └•CRS:        +proj=laea +lat_0=45 +lon_0=-100 +x_0=0 +y_0=0 +ellps=sphere +units=m +no_defs (projected) ## ├•formulation ## │├•name:        threatened_species ## ││├•objective:  minimum shortfall objective (`budget` = 5249.094) ## ││├•penalties: ## │││└•1:         none specified ## ││└•features:   "Recurvirostra americana (breeding)", … (396 total) ## ││ ├•targets:   relative targets (all equal to 0.8) ## ││ └•weights:   continuous values (between 0.0009 and 0.4276) ## │├•name:        public_interest_species ## ││├•objective:  minimum shortfall objective (`budget` = 5249.094) ## ││├•penalties: ## │││└•1:         none specified ## ││└•features:   "Recurvirostra americana (breeding)", … (396 total) ## ││ ├•targets:   relative targets (all equal to 0.8) ## ││ └•weights:   continuous values (between 0.01 and 5.492126) ## │├•name:        vulnerable_carbon ## ││├•objective:  maximum weighted sum objective (`budget` = 5249.094) ## ││├•penalties: ## │││└•1:         none specified ## ││└•features:   "vulnerable carbon" ## ││ ├•targets:   none specified ## ││ └•weights:   none specified ## │├•constraints: ## ││└•1:          locked in constraints (555 planning units) ## │└•decisions:   binary decision ## └•optimization ##  ├•approach:    epsilon constraint approach (`n_per_problem` = 3, `verbose` = TRUE) ##  └•solver:      gurobi solver (`gap` = 0.1, `time_limit` = 2147483647, `presolve` = 2, `threads` = 1, …) ## # ℹ Use `summary(...)` to see further details. # solve problem to generate prioritizations, # note that this will take a few minutes ms <- solve(mp, run_checks = FALSE, remove_duplicates = TRUE) # print the number of solutions generated print(length(ms)) ## [1] 7 # convert prioritizations to multi-layer raster, # and over-write values for existing protected areas to help with plotting sols <- terra::rast(ms) sols <- (sols * 2) - wa_locked_in  # plot the prioritizations as maps, wherein: # cells with a value of 0 are note selected # cells with a value of 1 are existing protected areas # cells with a value of 2 are priority areas plot(sols, nr = 3, axes = FALSE) # create table to store metrics for each prioritization, # note we will use NA values as place-holder values to start with metric_data <- data.frame(   name = names(ms),   threatened_species = NA,   public_interest_species = NA,   vulnerable_carbon = NA )  # calculate metric to evaluate prioritizations according to their # representation of threatened species (i.e., based on average # percent of each species' target that is met, weighted by extinction # probability) metric_data$threatened_species <- vapply(   ms,   FUN.VALUE = numeric(1),   function(x) {     mean(       eval_target_coverage_summary(p1, x)$relative_met *       100 *       wa_attr$extinction_prob     )   } )  # calculate metric to evaluate prioritizations according to their # representation of public interest species (i.e., based on average # percent of each species' target that is met, weighted by public # interest score) metric_data$public_interest_species <- vapply(   ms,   FUN.VALUE = numeric(1),   function(x) {     mean(       eval_target_coverage_summary(p1, x)$relative_met *       100 *       wa_attr$interest_score     )   } )  # calculate metric to evaluate prioritizations according to their # representation of vulnerable carbon (i.e., based on percentage of # total vulnerable carbon covered by priority areas) metric_data$vulnerable_carbon <- vapply(   ms,   FUN.VALUE = numeric(1),   function(x) {     mean(       eval_feature_representation_summary(p1, x)$relative_held * 100     )   } )  # print the metrics print(metric_data) ##         name threatened_species public_interest_species vulnerable_carbon ## 1 solution_1          0.3581032                48.35750          20.36066 ## 2 solution_2          0.3561382                51.65796          21.58850 ## 3 solution_3          0.2286733                34.95139          16.57897 ## 4 solution_4          0.2221509                50.82648          22.26093 ## 5 solution_5          0.2570307                49.14630          21.59461 ## 6 solution_6          0.3059821                50.89432          21.91841 ## 7 solution_7          0.3329506                50.81979          21.60339 # create plot to visualize trade-offs based on metrics p <-   ggplot(     data = metric_data,     aes(       x = threatened_species,       y = public_interest_species,       color = vulnerable_carbon     )   ) +   geom_point(size = 8) +   scale_color_viridis_c() +   labs(     x = "Threatened species representation (%)",     y = "Public interest species representation (%)",     color = "Vulnerable carbon representation (%)",   ) +   theme(     legend.position = c(0.99, 0.01),     legend.justification = c(1, 0)   )  # show plot print(p)

text: x prioritizr::multi_problem() object. n_per_problem integer number solutions generate per problem x. Note include solutions represent optimizing exclusively objective individually (.e., extreme points Pareto frontier). particular, total number solutions equal \(o + n^(o - 1)\), \(o\) number problems x \(n\) equal n_per_problem. example, x three problems n_per_problem = 4, 19 solutions generated. verbose logical (TRUE/FALSE) progress generating solutions displayed? Defaults TRUE.

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_epconstraint_approach
code: # in this example, we aim to identify a set of planning units that will # not exceed a particular budget and meet objectives for # (i) representing species that are important for ecosystem # functioning (hereafter, keystone species) and (ii) representing species # that have high social or cultural value (hereafter, iconic species)  # load packages library(prioritizr) library(terra) #> terra 1.9.27 #>  #> Attaching package: ‘terra’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:prioritizr’: #>  #>     ncell  # import data con_cost <- get_sim_pu_raster() keystone_spp <- get_sim_features()[[1:3]] iconic_spp <- get_sim_features()[[4:5]]  # define a total conservation budget (20% of total cost) budget <- terra::global(con_cost, "sum", na.rm = TRUE)[[1]] * 0.2  # define a single-objective problem for the keystone species objective p1 <-   problem(con_cost, keystone_spp) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.4) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # define a single-objective problem for the iconic species objective p2 <-   problem(con_cost, iconic_spp) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.8) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # now create multi-objective problem with epsilon-constraint approach, # with settings to generate (i) a solution for each objective # and (ii) three additional solutions that represent a varying # degree of trade-offs between these objectives mp <-   multi_problem(keystone_obj = p1, iconic_obj = p2) %>%   add_epconstraint_approach(n_per_problem = 4, verbose = TRUE) %>%   add_default_solver(gap = 0, verbose = FALSE)  # solve problem ms <- solve(mp) #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                 | 3/6 |  50% | ETA: 2s #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■       | 5/6 |  83% | ETA: 1s #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■  | 6/6 | 100% | ETA: 0s  # convert solutions to a multi-layer raster object sol <- rast(ms)  # assign names to each solution names(sol) <- c(   "Keystone species", "Iconic species",   paste("trade-off", seq_len(nlyr(sol) - 2)) )  # plot solutions to view selection of priority areas, # here, the first two maps show solutions for best achieving # each of the objectives, and the subsequent maps show solutions # that aim to reach varying degrees of compromise between the objectives plot(sol, axes = FALSE)   # extract objective values for the solutions obj_matrix <- attributes(ms)$objective  # print the objective values print(obj_matrix) #>            keystone_obj iconic_obj #> solution_1     1.559674   1.563225 #> solution_2     1.755484   1.463960 #> solution_3     1.680262   1.483804 #> solution_4     1.633465   1.503609 #> solution_5     1.600177   1.523389 #> solution_6     1.574416   1.543313  # plot the objectives values to visualize trade-offs # (note that smaller values are better because these objectives seek to # minimize representation shortfalls) plot(   obj_matrix,   main = "Trade-offs between objectives",   xlab = "Keystone objective (shortfall)",   ylab = "Iconic objective (shortfall)" )

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
text: x prioritizr::multi_problem() object. n_per_problem integer number solutions attempt generate per problem x. Note number include solutions represent optimizing exclusively objective individually (.e., extreme points Pareto frontier). particular, total number solutions approach attempts generate equal \(o + n^{(o - 1)}\), \(o\) number problems x \(n\) equal n_per_problem. example, x three problems n_per_problem = 4, approach attempt generate 19 solutions. Due mathematical details approach (see ), guaranteed generate solution attempt often generate fewer solutions attempts. verbose logical (TRUE/FALSE) progress generating solutions displayed? Defaults TRUE.

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
text: updated prioritizr::multi_problem() object approach added .

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
text: multi-objective optimization approach especially useful characterizing full range trade-offs objectives well-defined order importance among objectives planning exercise. general, recommend using approach characterizing full range trade-offs objectives procedures automatically account best worst possible levels achievement optimizing different objectives. Due procedures, approach require user specify trade-parameters (e.g., unlike rel_tol prioritizr::add_hier_approach()) instead requires user specify parameter reflects maximum number desired solutions. Furthermore, approach sensitive differences scale among different objectives (unlike weighted sum approach, prioritizr::add_wtd_sum_approach(); see Das Dennis 1997 details), can readily applied wide range objectives.

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
text: approach can expressed mathematically set objectives associated problem() objects x. Let \(O\) denote set objectives (indexed \(o\)). brevity, assume objectives ideally maximized sorted order priority (per priority), objective highest priority \(o=1\), objective second highest priority \(o=2\), . Although approach can applied arbitrary number objectives, assume \(O\) three objectives explaining approach. Also, let \(f_o(x)\) denote objective function objective \(o \O\), \(x\) represents decision variables calculating objective values (e.g., planning unit selection status values). Additionally, let \(S\) represent set (region) feasible values \(x\) based constraints objectives (e.g., first problem x locked constraints second problem locked constraints, \(S\) account locked locked constraints). Given terminology, approach starts formulating set multi-objective optimization problems generate solution focuses primarily objective whilst accounting objectives (hereafter, extreme points). Note multi-objective optimization problems solved using hierarchical approach optimization relative tolerance values set zero (see prioritizr::add_hier_approach()) details). Multi-objective problem first extreme point: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_1(x), f_2(x), f_3(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S $$ Multi-objective problem second extreme point: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_2(x), f_1(x), f_3(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S $$ Multi-objective problem third extreme point: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_3(x), f_1(x), f_2(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S $$ solving problems generate solutions, let \(b_o\) denote best objective value objective. Also let \(w_o\) denote worst value objective. objective – except first objective – generate sequence (evenly distributed) numbers ranging worst objective value (per \(w_o\)) best objective value (per \(b_o\)), based predefined number values (per n_per_problem). example, second objective \(w_o = 1\) \(b_o = 5\) n_per_problem = 3, sequence numbers objective equal \(\{2, 3, 4\}\). Similarly, third objective \(w_o = 5\) \(b_o = 15\) n_per_problem = 3, sequence numbers objective equal \(\{7.5, 10.0, 12.5\}\). use sequences numbers generate set numbers represents possible combinations numbers objective. example, considered previous sequences (second objective) \(\{2, 3, 4\}\) (third objective) \(\{7.5, 10.0, 12.5\}\), generate following set nine combinations numbers: \(\{2, 7.5\}\), \(\{3, 7.5\}\), \(\{4, 7.5\}\), \(\{2, 10\}\), \(\{3, 10\}\), \(\{4, 10\}\), \(\{2, 12.5\}\), \(\{3, 12.5\}\), \(\{4, 12.5\}\). brevity, let \(v_{io}\) denote number objective \(o\) 'th sequence (e.g,. number third objective first combination \(v_{12} = 7.5\)). Given , approach involves formulating optimization problem based combination numbers solving . Problem based first combination: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_1(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S \\ f_2(x) >= v_{11}, \\ f_3(x) >= v_{12}, \\ $$ Problem based second combination: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_1(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S \\ f_2(x) >= v_{21}, \\ f_3(x) >= v_{22}, \\ $$ Problem based third combination: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_1(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S \\ f_2(x) >= v_{31}, \\ f_3(x) >= v_{32}, \\ $$ Problem based 'th combination: $$ \mathit{Maximize} \space f_1(x) \\ \mathit{subject \space \space} x \S \\ f_2(x) >= v_{i1}, \\ f_3(x) >= v_{i2}, \\ $$ manner, approach sequentially formulated solves optimization problems attempted generate solution combination numbers. resulting set solutions represent varying degrees compromise different objectives. Note may impossible generate solution combinations numbers, approach may actually generate solution every combination. Finally, approach returns solutions successfully generated (including extreme points).

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
text: Das Dennis JE (1997) closer look drawbacks minimizing weighted sums objectives Pareto set generation multicriteria optimization problems. Structural Optimization, 14: 63–69. Eichfelder G (2008) Adaptive Scalarization Methods Multiobjective Optimization. Springer Berlin Heidelberg.

title: Add epsilon constraint approach — add_eps_constraint_approach
code: # in this example, we aim to identify a set of planning units that will # not exceed a particular budget and meet objectives for # (i) representing species that are important for ecosystem # functioning (hereafter, keystone species) and (ii) representing species # that have high social or cultural value (hereafter, iconic species)  # load packages library(prioritizr) library(terra) #> terra 1.9.27 #>  #> Attaching package: ‘terra’ #> The following object is masked from ‘package:prioritizr’: #>  #>     ncell  # import data con_cost <- get_sim_pu_raster() keystone_spp <- get_sim_features()[[1:3]] iconic_spp <- get_sim_features()[[4:5]]  # define a total conservation budget (20% of total cost) budget <- terra::global(con_cost, "sum", na.rm = TRUE)[[1]] * 0.2  # define a single-objective problem for the keystone species objective p1 <-   problem(con_cost, keystone_spp) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.4) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # define a single-objective problem for the iconic species objective p2 <-   problem(con_cost, iconic_spp) %>%   add_min_shortfall_objective(budget) %>%   add_relative_targets(0.8) %>%   add_binary_decisions()  # now create multi-objective problem with epsilon-constraint approach, # with settings to generate (i) a solution for each objective # and (ii) three additional solutions that represent a varying # degree of trade-offs between these objectives mp <-   multi_problem(keystone_obj = p1, iconic_obj = p2) %>%   add_eps_constraint_approach(n_per_problem = 4, verbose = TRUE) %>%   add_default_solver(gap = 0, verbose = FALSE)  # solve problem ms <- solve(mp) #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                 | 3/6 |  50% | ETA: 2s #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■            | 4/6 |  67% | ETA: 1s #> Generating solutions ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■  | 6/6 | 100% | ETA: 0s  # convert solutions to a multi-layer raster object sol <- rast(ms)  # assign names to each solution names(sol) <- c(   "Keystone species", "Iconic species",   paste("trade-off", seq_len(nlyr(sol) - 2)) )  # plot solutions to view selection of priority areas, # here, the first two maps show solutions for best achieving # each of the objectives, and the subsequent maps show solutions # that aim to reach varying degrees of compromise between the objectives plot(sol, axes = FALSE)   # extract objective values for the solutions obj_matrix <- attributes(ms)$objective  # print the objective values print(obj_matrix) #>            keystone_obj iconic_obj #> solution_1     1.559674   1.563225 #> solution_2     1.755484   1.463960 #> solution_3     1.680262   1.483804 #> solution_4     1.633465   1.503609 #> solution_5     1.600177   1.523389 #> solution_6     1.574416   1.543313  # plot the objectives values to visualize trade-offs # (note that smaller values are better because these objectives seek to # minimize representation shortfalls) plot(   obj_matrix,   main = "Trade-offs between objectives",   xlab = "Keystone objective (shortfall)",   ylab = "Iconic objective (shortfall)" )

what: moec.prioritizr: Multi-Objective Prioritization with the Epsilon-Constraint Approach — moec.prioritizr
title: moec.prioritizr: Multi-Objective Prioritization with the Epsilon-Constraint Approach — moec.prioritizr
text: Systematic conservation planning framework identify priority areas conservation management (Giakoumi et al. 2025). Briefly, priority areas identified formulating mathematical optimization problem based conservation objectives requirements using optimization algorithms generate solutions. help account multiple objectives, moec.prioritizr package provides implementation epsilon-constraint approach multi-objective optimization (Eichfelder 2008). package designed plugin prioritizr package – general purpose package systematic conservation planning (Hanson et al. 2025) – provide enhanced functionality. using multi-objective optimization approaches, conservation scientists practitioners can explore trade-offs identify solutions represent desirable compromise among multiple objectives (Neubert et al. 2025).
